refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve end-to-end teleoperation communication latency with p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks specificity on measurement points, network conditions, and safety context. Refinement clarifies that latency is measured end-to-end (sensor-to-actuator), establishes the statistical percentile (p95) as the acceptance criterion, and anchors the requirement to safety-critical remote operation. This prevents ambiguity in what 'compatible with safe remote operation' means.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure round-trip latency from operator input (tele-operator console) through network to equipment response (vehicle actuator movement) under representative network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss). Collect 1000+ samples across multiple operational scenarios and verify p95 ≤ 150 ms. Validate against safety analysis to confirm latency margin is adequate for operator reaction time and fail-safe behavior.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall measure and report end-to-end latency from operator input to vehicle response.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall maintain network communication link with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall implement latency monitoring and alert mechanisms to notify operators when latency exceeds safe thresholds.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall provide graceful degradation or safe shutdown if latency exceeds operational limits.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement edge computing with local autonomy fallback; minimize round-trip network hops via regional gateway architecture; use UDP-based low-latency protocol stack.

DP-B: Deploy dedicated low-latency network infrastructure (5G private network or dedicated fiber); implement hardware-accelerated packet processing; use real-time OS scheduling on vehicle and operator console.

DP-C: Hybrid approach: combine regional edge gateways (DP-A) with network QoS prioritization and latency monitoring; implement predictive command buffering on vehicle; use adaptive codec selection to reduce payload size.
DP-D: Centralized cloud-based teleoperation with optimized CDN routing; implement client-side prediction and server-side state synchronization; use TCP with aggressive timeout tuning.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid approach combining regional edge gateways with network QoS prioritization, latency monitoring, predictive command buffering on vehicle, and adaptive codec selection.

potential_sources: Coupling between latency performance (DP-C) and network infrastructure availability (external dependency on agreed network conditions).
Coupling between edge gateway placement (DP-C) and vehicle communication module design (must support dual-path: direct + gateway).
Coupling between adaptive codec selection and Safety Services (must ensure safety-critical telemetry is never compressed).
coupling_check.notes: DP-C is selected because it balances latency reduction (edge gateways), robustness (QoS + monitoring), and graceful degradation (predictive buffering). It avoids over-dependence on infrastructure (DP-B) or under-specification of safety (DP-D). Coupling risks are manageable through interface contracts and safety analysis.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Agreed network conditions are defined and stable (e.g., 4G LTE with <50 ms backbone latency, <5% packet loss).
ASSUMPTION: Regional edge gateways can be deployed and maintained within project scope.
RISK: If network conditions degrade beyond assumptions, p95 ≤ 150 ms may not be achievable; mitigation requires fallback to local autonomy or operation suspension.
RISK: Predictive command buffering may cause operator-perceived lag if prediction model is inaccurate; requires tuning and operator training.
RISK: Adaptive codec selection complexity may introduce latency variance; requires careful implementation and testing.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify in DP-C the specific latency budget allocation: (1) operator input capture (5 ms), (2) network transmission (80 ms), (3) vehicle processing (30 ms), (4) actuator response (20 ms), (5) feedback loop (15 ms). This enables targeted optimization and risk mitigation at each stage.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end latency performance, including operator console communication, network link management, edge gateway coordination, vehicle command processing, and latency monitoring. It integrates with Vehicle (for actuator control and sensor feedback), Safety Services (for latency-based safety alerts), and external Network Link (for QoS and routing).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode selection) from tele-operator console; must be processed with minimal latency.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Sends vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, actuator status) back to operator console; latency of this feedback loop is critical for operator situational awareness.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and alerts from Safety Services (e.g., latency threshold exceeded, geofence violation); sends latency monitoring data to Safety Services for real-time safety assessment.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Manages communication with network infrastructure (edge gateways, QoS policies, routing); monitors network latency, jitter, and packet loss; adapts codec and buffering strategies based on network state.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container but does not own latency performance directly; latency is a property of Teleoperation subsystem. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility and obscure ownership.

Vehicle owns actuator control and sensor feedback but does not own network communication or operator console integration. Latency performance spans both Vehicle and network domains; assigning solely to Vehicle would miss network-side optimizations (edge gateways, QoS).

Teleoperation is the natural owner of end-to-end latency performance. It encompasses operator console, network communication, edge gateway coordination, and vehicle command/feedback processing. All four external interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are primary concerns of Teleoperation. High containment, minimal diffusion.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerts but does not own latency performance itself. Latency is an input to safety decisions, not a Safety Services responsibility. Assigning here would create circular dependency.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of FR-NGHE-008 because: (1) it directly controls operator console, network communication, and vehicle command processing; (2) all four external interfaces are primary inputs/outputs of Teleoperation; (3) latency optimization strategies (edge gateways, QoS, adaptive codecs, predictive buffering) are naturally contained within Teleoperation; (4) ownership is clear and unambiguous, enabling accountability and testing.
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module may become large and complex if it also owns operator UI, network protocol stack, and vehicle communication driver. Mitigation: decompose Teleoperation into sub-modules (operator console, network gateway, vehicle link) at next level.

RISK: Latency performance depends on Vehicle module's actuator response time and sensor sampling rate. Teleoperation must interface with Vehicle via well-defined contracts (e.g., maximum actuator latency, sensor update frequency). Mitigation: establish interface SLAs between Teleoperation and Vehicle.

RISK: Network Link is external to NGHE Solution; Teleoperation cannot fully control network latency. Mitigation: define agreed network conditions as a constraint; implement monitoring and graceful degradation when network conditions degrade.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish explicit interface contracts between Teleoperation and Vehicle: (1) Vehicle shall respond to Tasking commands within 30 ms; (2) Vehicle shall provide Telemetry updates at ≥ 10 Hz; (3) Vehicle shall support latency monitoring via SafetyAlert interface. This clarifies the boundary between Teleoperation and Vehicle and enables independent testing.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid edge gateways + QoS + monitoring + adaptive codecs) directly addresses the refined FR requirement for p95 ≤ 150 ms latency. The DP is specific, measurable, and includes monitoring mechanisms to verify compliance. No independence violations detected; DP-C is a coherent solution concept.

diffusion_risks: DP-C requires coordination across multiple architectural layers (operator console, network gateways, vehicle). Teleoperation module must own all these layers to avoid diffusion. Mitigation: define clear sub-module boundaries within Teleoperation at next decomposition level.

Adaptive codec selection (part of DP-C) may require coordination with Safety Services to ensure safety-critical telemetry is never compressed. Mitigation: establish SafetyAlert interface contract specifying which telemetry streams are safety-critical and exempt from compression.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment (0.95) for DP-C. All key design decisions (edge gateway placement, QoS policies, codec selection, predictive buffering) are naturally owned by Teleoperation. External dependencies (Network Link, Vehicle) are managed via well-defined interfaces.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-008 is feasible within Teleoperation module scope. Latency can be measured end-to-end using instrumentation at operator console (input timestamp) and vehicle (output timestamp). Testing can be conducted in lab (simulated network) and field (real network conditions). Acceptance criteria (p95 ≤ 150 ms) is objective and measurable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns latency performance. Sub-allocations to Vehicle (actuator response time), Network Link (transmission latency), and Safety Services (latency monitoring) are defined via interface contracts. Ownership boundaries are unambiguous.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-C to specify latency budget allocation across operator console (5 ms), network transmission (80 ms), vehicle processing (30 ms), actuator response (20 ms), feedback loop (15 ms). This enables targeted optimization and risk mitigation.
Establish interface SLAs between Teleoperation and Vehicle: maximum actuator latency, sensor update frequency, latency monitoring support.
Define agreed network conditions as a formal constraint document: bandwidth, latency, jitter, packet loss, coverage area, failover mechanisms.
Clarify which telemetry streams are safety-critical and exempt from adaptive codec compression (via SafetyAlert interface contract).

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-008 is not atomic; it encompasses multiple sub-concerns (network communication, vehicle command processing, latency monitoring, safety integration). The refined FR explicitly identifies four sub-FRs that require further decomposition. DP-C is sound and well-mapped to Teleoperation module, but the module itself is complex and requires sub-level decomposition to manage risk and ensure clarity. Proceeding to next level of functional decomposition is necessary to allocate responsibilities within Teleoperation (e.g., operator console, network gateway, vehicle link, monitoring) and establish interface contracts with Vehicle and Safety Services.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall measure and report end-to-end latency from operator input to vehicle response.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall maintain network communication link with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall implement latency monitoring and alert mechanisms to notify operators when latency exceeds safe thresholds.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall provide graceful degradation or safe shutdown if latency exceeds operational limits.

assumptions: Agreed network conditions are defined and stable (e.g., 4G LTE with <50 ms backbone latency, <5% packet loss).
Regional edge gateways can be deployed and maintained within project scope.
Vehicle module can support dual-path communication (direct + gateway) and provide latency monitoring data.
Safety Services can enforce latency-based safety policies and provide real-time alerts.
Operator training and procedures account for predictive command buffering behavior.
open_risks: Network conditions may degrade beyond assumptions; fallback to local autonomy or operation suspension required.
Predictive command buffering may cause operator-perceived lag if prediction model is inaccurate.
Adaptive codec selection complexity may introduce latency variance.
Teleoperation module may become large and complex; sub-module decomposition required to manage complexity.
Interface contracts between Teleoperation and Vehicle must be established to ensure latency SLAs are met.
evidence_needed: Network latency characterization under agreed conditions (baseline measurement).
Vehicle actuator response time specification (from Vehicle module owner).
Safety analysis confirming that p95 ≤ 150 ms latency is adequate for safe remote operation.
Operator feedback on acceptable latency and predictive buffering behavior (from tele-operator stakeholder).
Edge gateway deployment plan and network QoS policy documentation.
Latency monitoring and alerting mechanism design (to be detailed at next decomposition level).
